summary: Programming language debates are a waste of time because they focus on irrelevant technical minutiae rather than what actually matters: whether users are happy with what you build and whether your business succeeds. The choice of programming language is almost never a factor in commercial success, and end-users don't care about your tech stack.
key_points: Programming language performance claims are meaningless without specifying use cases - 'slow' is always relative
Python may be 'the second best language at everything' but has powered companies to 9-figure exits
End-users don't care about your programming language choice
Language debates are about winning 'brownie points with other nerds' rather than building successful products
Pick the tools that work for your situation rather than chasing theoretical ideals
money_quotes: The choice of programming language is almost always irrelevant to an organization's commercial success.
I often quip Python is the 'second best language at everything.'
You need to upskill your business sense if you're trying to differentiate your product or service based on the underlying programming language or tech stack.
Cool if you're trying to win brownie points with other nerds. Debate about your tech stack all day long. The rest of the world doesn't care.
Slow is relative to the use case, so making a universal claim about speed is a red herring.
key_insight: Stop debating programming languages and start focusing on whether your product actually solves problems for users - that's the only metric that matters.
